section.title: Disaster from the North
section.sectionRationale: The boiling pot vision of chapter 1 now becomes an urgent battle alarm. Jeremiah sounds the trumpet, describes an invading nation as a lion and a scorching wind, feels the coming disaster in his own body, and finally sees in vision the undoing of creation itself, judgment so severe it reverses Genesis.
section.thesis: The LORD is bringing an unstoppable invader from the north against Judah's covenant unfaithfulness, a judgment so total that Jeremiah pictures the created order itself unraveling back toward formlessness, yet even here the LORD will not make a full end.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the trumpet sounded and cities warned to flee; the scorching wind and the besieging nation described; Jeremiah's own anguish at what he sees; and the vision of uncreation, the earth returning to formless void.

units.label: Sound the alarm
units.note: the shofar, the signal flag, the lion from his thicket

verses.text:
Announce in Judah, proclaim in Jerusalem, and say: 
“Blow the {{shofar|ram's horn}} throughout the land. Cry aloud and say, ‘Assemble yourselves and let us flee to the fortified cities.’
Raise a signal flag toward Zion. Seek refuge! Do not delay! For I am bringing disaster from the north, and terrible destruction.
A lion has gone up from his thicket, and a destroyer of nations has set out. He has left his lair to lay waste your land. Your cities will be reduced to ruins and lie uninhabited.
So put on sackcloth, mourn and wail, for the fierce anger of the LORD has not turned away from us.”
“In that day,” declares the LORD, “the king and officials will lose their courage. The priests will tremble in fear, and the prophets will be astounded.”
Then I said, “Ah, Lord GOD, how completely You have deceived this people and Jerusalem by saying, ‘You will have peace,’ while a sword is at our throats.”

units.label: The scorching wind
units.note: an army like clouds and whirlwind

verses.text: At that time it will be said to this people and to Jerusalem, “A searing wind from the barren heights in the desert blows toward the daughter of My people, but not to winnow or to sift;
a wind too strong for that comes from Me. Now I also pronounce judgments against them.”
Behold, he advances like the clouds, his chariots like the whirlwind. His horses are swifter than eagles. Woe to us, for we are ruined!
Wash the evil from your heart, O Jerusalem, so that you may be saved. How long will you harbor wicked thoughts within you?
For a voice resounds from Dan, proclaiming disaster from the hills of Ephraim.
Warn the nations now! Proclaim to Jerusalem: “A besieging army comes from a distant land; they raise their voices against the cities of Judah.
They surround her like men guarding a field, because she has rebelled against Me,” declares the LORD.
“Your ways and deeds have brought this upon you. This is your punishment; how bitter it is, because it pierces to the heart!”

units.label: Jeremiah's anguish
units.note: my anguish, I cannot be silent

verses.text: My anguish, my anguish! I writhe in pain! Oh, the pain in my chest! My heart pounds within me; I cannot be silent. For I have heard the sound of the horn, the alarm of battle.
Disaster after disaster is proclaimed, for the whole land is laid waste. My tents are destroyed in an instant, my curtains in a moment.
How long must I see the signal flag and hear the sound of the horn?
“For My people are fools; they have not known Me. They are foolish children, without understanding. They are skilled in doing evil, but they know not how to do good.”

units.label: Uncreation
units.note: formless and void, no light, no man, no birds

verses.text: I looked at the earth, and it was {{tohu_vabohu|formless and void}}; I looked to the heavens, and they had no light.

I looked at the mountains, and behold, they were quaking; 
all the hills were swaying.
I looked, and no man was left; all the birds of the air had fled.
I looked, and the fruitful land was a desert. All its cities were torn down before the LORD, before His fierce anger.

For this is what the LORD says: 
“The whole land will be desolate, but I will not finish its destruction.
Therefore the earth will mourn and the heavens above will grow dark. I have spoken, I have planned, and I will not relent or turn back.”
Every city flees at the sound of the horseman and archer. They enter the thickets and climb among the rocks. Every city is abandoned; no inhabitant is left.

And you, O devastated one, what will you do, though you dress yourself in scarlet, though you adorn yourself with gold jewelry, though you enlarge your eyes with paint? You adorn yourself in vain; your lovers despise you; they want to take your life.

For I hear a cry like a woman in labor, a cry of anguish like one bearing her first child, the cry of the Daughter of Zion gasping for breath, stretching out her hands to say, “Woe is me, for my soul faints before the murderers!”

shofar.gloss: A ram's horn trumpet, used both to summon Israel to worship and to warn of approaching war. Its repeated sounding through this passage marks the shift from Jeremiah's private call to public, national alarm.

tohu_vabohu.gloss: Formless and void, the exact phrase used of the earth before creation in Genesis 1:2. Jeremiah deliberately reuses it here, picturing covenant judgment as a kind of undoing of creation itself, the land returned toward the chaos from which God first spoke order.

basic.context: Jeremiah sounds the battle alarm: an unnamed invader from the north is coming like a lion, like a scorching desert wind, like storm clouds, and no one, not king, priest, or prophet, will keep their nerve. Jeremiah himself is not a detached announcer; he feels the coming disaster in his own body, and in a devastating vision he sees the created world itself begin to unravel back toward the darkness and formlessness of Genesis 1:2.
basic.hermeneutics: This is prophetic poetry describing a real coming military disaster, almost certainly the Babylonian invasions that would culminate in 586 BC, using cosmic and creational imagery to convey the theological weight of covenant judgment. The hard truth to sit with is verse 27, God will not make a full end, judgment here is real and severe, described in the most catastrophic language available, yet even at its worst it stops short of total annihilation.
basic.originalLanguages: The shofar sounds again and again, moving the alarm from private oracle to public emergency. When Jeremiah looks at the devastated land, he sees it as tohu vabohu, formless and void, the identical phrase used of the earth in Genesis 1:2 before God spoke light and order into being, so that covenant judgment is pictured as creation undone.
basic.typology: The reversal of creation here anticipates its opposite: the new creation promised throughout Scripture, where God speaks light, order, and life back into a ruined world through Christ. Jeremiah's own anguish, feeling in his body the judgment his people refuse to feel, anticipates the greater weeping and grief of Christ over Jerusalem.
basic.christ: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem in words that echo this very theme, if only you had known the things that make for peace, Luke 19:41-44, standing in the line of Jeremiah as a prophet who feels covenant judgment in His own body and grieves over a city's refusal to turn. Where Jeremiah's vision ends in near-total undoing, the gospel promises the reversal of that undoing, a new heaven and new earth, in Christ.
basic.god: God is both the author of this judgment, I have spoken, I have planned, and I will not relent, and the one who limits it, I will not finish its destruction. His anger against covenant unfaithfulness is severe enough to picture the unmaking of creation, yet His mercy still holds back total annihilation, keeping a future open beyond the disaster.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 1:2 supplies the exact phrase, formless and void, that Jeremiah reuses to describe judgment. Luke 19:41-44 has Jesus weep over Jerusalem's coming destruction in language that echoes Jeremiah's own anguish. Zephaniah 1:2-3 pictures a similarly cosmic undoing of creation in judgment. Revelation 21:1-5 answers this undoing with the promise of a fully remade heaven and earth.
basic.oneStory: This is the alarm before the storm, a prophet who feels in his own body the judgment coming on a people who will not feel it themselves, pictured as creation itself unraveling. That undoing is not the last word of Scripture; it points forward to the new creation God will finally accomplish through Christ, where light, order, and life are spoken back into a ruined world for good.

context.literary: The unit alternates between third-person prophetic announcement and first-person prophetic lament, a structure unique to Jeremiah among the prophets, in which the messenger's own emotional and physical anguish becomes part of the message itself, climaxing in a four-fold I looked vision (4:23-26) that systematically reverses the order of Genesis 1.
context.historical: The unnamed enemy from the north almost certainly refers to Babylon, whose armies would eventually besiege and destroy Jerusalem in 586 BC, though at the time of this oracle, likely under Josiah or early Jehoiakim, Babylon's rise to dominance was not yet fully evident, lending the warning an unsettling, still-unfolding quality.
context.canonical: The deliberate echo of Genesis 1:2 places this judgment oracle within the Bible's largest frame, creation and its unraveling through sin, anticipating the New Testament's promise of new creation as the ultimate reversal of exactly this kind of covenant judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn Judah in the most vivid possible terms of an imminent, God-ordained military catastrophe, using the language of uncreation to show that covenant unfaithfulness has consequences as serious as unraveling the created order itself, while still holding out that God will not utterly destroy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific, historically located coming judgment on Jeremiah's Judah; it is not a prescription for how God deals with every nation in every era. The enduring truth is that persistent covenant unfaithfulness invites real judgment, and that even severe judgment operates within limits set by God's own restraint.

debates.question: Is the reversal of creation in 4:23-26 a literal cosmic event or a poetic depiction of the devastation of the land?

views.label: Poetic hyperbole describing total devastation
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern prophetic literature regularly used cosmic collapse imagery to convey the totality of a coming military and social catastrophe, without asserting literal astronomical or cosmological events; the focus is the felt totality of the disaster for those living through it.

views.label: A genuine theological statement about judgment undoing creation
views.case: Others take the deliberate echo of Genesis 1:2 as a serious theological claim that covenant judgment really does function as a partial undoing of God's created order for that land, not mere hyperbole, since sin's consequences genuinely unravel the order God intended.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that the judgment is severe enough to be described in the most extreme available terms, and that its limitation, God will not make a full end, is the crucial qualifier that keeps hope alive beyond the disaster.

study.originalLanguages: The shofar's repeated blast turns Jeremiah's private call into urgent, public alarm, a summons the whole nation must hear. When Jeremiah looks out at the devastation to come, he sees tohu vabohu, formless and void, the very words used of the uncreated earth in Genesis 1:2, so that covenant judgment is deliberately pictured as creation's undoing, not merely military defeat.

typology.type: The earth returning to formless and void, Jeremiah 4:23
typology.fulfillment: Answered and reversed in the promise of a new heaven and new earth, where God's ordering, life-giving word is spoken again in full, Revelation 21:1-5.

typology.type: Jeremiah's bodily anguish over coming judgment, Jeremiah 4:19
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Christ's weeping over Jerusalem's coming judgment, Luke 19:41-44, the greater prophet who grieves over the city that will not know the things that make for peace.

study.christ: Jesus stands in the line of Jeremiah when He weeps over Jerusalem, if you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace, Luke 19:41-44, feeling in His own person the weight of covenant judgment His people would not feel for themselves. Where Jeremiah's vision pictures creation unraveling under judgment, the gospel answers that undoing with the promise of new creation, God's ordering word spoken again fully and finally through the risen Christ.
study.god: God both ordains this judgment, I have spoken, I have planned, and I will not relent, and limits it, I will not finish its destruction, holding together real, severe justice with real, abiding mercy. His anger is not out of control even when it is pictured as cosmic in scope; it operates within bounds He Himself sets and never exceeds.

crossReferences.note: Supplies the exact phrase, formless and void, that Jeremiah applies to judgment, framing it as a kind of uncreation.
Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment in a way that echoes Jeremiah's own bodily anguish over his people.
Pictures a similarly total, cosmic-scale judgment using creation-reversal imagery.
Answers the uncreation of judgment with the full and final promise of a new heaven and new earth.

study.oneStory: This is the alarm sounded before the storm breaks, a prophet who feels in his own body a judgment his people refuse to feel, pictured as creation itself unraveling toward formlessness. That undoing is not Scripture's final word; it points forward to the new creation God finally accomplishes through Christ, when light, order, and life are spoken back into a ruined world forever.

quiz.q: What image does Jeremiah use to describe the coming invader in verse 7?

options.text: A lion that has gone up from his thicket, a destroyer of nations.
options.note: Verse 7 pictures the invader as a lion leaving its lair to lay waste the land.

options.text: A gentle shepherd bringing peace.
options.note: The passage describes violent destruction, the opposite of a gentle shepherd image.

options.text: A farmer bringing a harvest.
options.note: This is not the imagery used; the passage describes military devastation, not agricultural blessing.

options.text: A trading caravan from the east.
options.note: The invader is explicitly military and destructive, not a peaceful trading party.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah personally respond to the coming disaster in verses 19-21?

options.text: With physical anguish, writhing in pain and unable to stay silent.
options.note: Verse 19 shows Jeremiah feeling the coming judgment in his own body, not merely announcing it dispassionately.

options.text: With detached indifference toward his people's fate.
options.note: The text shows the opposite: deep personal anguish, not indifference.

options.text: With joy that his enemies will be punished.
options.note: Jeremiah's response is anguish and grief, not celebration.

options.text: By refusing to speak of it any further.
options.note: He explicitly says, I cannot be silent, continuing to proclaim the message despite his pain.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah see when he looks at the earth in verse 23?

options.text: The earth as formless and void, using the exact phrase from Genesis 1:2.
options.note: This deliberate echo pictures judgment as a kind of undoing of creation itself.

options.text: A newly built and thriving city.
options.note: The vision describes desolation and undoing, not new prosperity.

options.text: A peaceful and fruitful garden.
options.note: The vision is one of devastation, mountains quaking, no man, no birds, not a flourishing garden.

options.text: An image confined only to the temple building.
options.note: The vision describes the whole land and even the heavens, not merely the temple structure.

quiz.q: Despite the severity of this judgment, what limit does God set on it in verse 27?

options.text: The whole land will be desolate, but He will not make a full end.
options.note: Even amid the most severe judgment imagery in the passage, God explicitly limits the destruction.

options.text: The judgment will be reversed immediately with no consequences.
options.note: The passage describes real, severe desolation; it is limited, not avoided.

options.text: Only the northern tribes will be affected, not Judah.
options.note: The judgment explicitly targets Jerusalem and Judah throughout the passage.

options.text: The limit applies only to the enemy nation, not Judah itself.
options.note: The stated limit, I will not finish its destruction, refers to the land and people just described, Judah itself.

quiz.q: How does this passage anticipate the New Testament?

options.text: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem's coming judgment, echoing Jeremiah's own anguish, and Revelation promises a new creation reversing this undoing.
options.note: Luke 19:41-44 and Revelation 21:1-5 both connect meaningfully to this passage's themes of prophetic grief and creation's undoing and restoration.

options.text: It has no meaningful connection to the New Testament.
options.note: The connections to Jesus' weeping over Jerusalem and to new creation are direct and substantial.

options.text: It only relates to end-times prophecy about literal astronomical events.
options.note: The connection is theological and typological, about judgment and new creation, not primarily literal astronomy.

options.text: It is fulfilled entirely within the Old Testament and has no further significance.
options.note: While it addresses Jeremiah's own historical moment, its creation-reversal imagery carries forward into New Testament hope of new creation.

questions: Jeremiah feels the coming judgment in his own body rather than announcing it with detachment. Where do you tend to keep hard truths at arm's length instead of letting them affect you honestly?
The invading disaster is described as unstoppable, like a lion, like a scorching wind. What in your life feels unstoppable right now, and how does trusting God change how you face it?
Jeremiah's vision pictures creation itself unraveling under judgment. Where have you seen sin's consequences unravel something good that God originally intended for flourishing?
Even at its most severe, God says He will not make a full end. Where do you need to trust God's restraint and mercy in the middle of real consequences you are facing?
Jesus later wept over Jerusalem in words that echo this very passage. What would it look like for you to grieve honestly over sin, your own or your community's, the way both Jeremiah and Jesus did?

translationNotes.note: Jeremiah's charge, You have deceived this people, is a striking accusation against God; many read this as Jeremiah voicing the people's own confusion at the false prophets' message of peace, rather than a literal claim that God lied.
Formless and void, tohu wa-bohu, occurs in the Hebrew Bible as this exact paired phrase only here and in Genesis 1:2, making the allusion to creation's original state unmistakable to Hebrew readers.

The address to the devastated one dressed in scarlet pictures Jerusalem as a woman applying cosmetics and finery in a futile attempt to attract the very lovers, foreign powers, who will bring about her ruin.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit this session.
